preparation_method: assembled with peanut sauce and kecap manis

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Ketoprak weighs approximately 350g, comprising lontong rice cake (~80g), rice vermicelli (~40g), fried tofu (~60g), bean sprouts and cucumber (~100g), and peanut sauce with kecap manis (~70g).

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Ketoprak
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 food composition table for Ketoprak. This is an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods and Lontong Rice Cake — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed analogous estimates
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ketoprak as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~65 is estimated from the weighted contribution of its dominant starchy components: lontong compressed rice cake (GI ~82, analogous to white rice cake per University of Sydney GI Database) and rice vermicelli (GI ~58, University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles). The peanut sauce fat component moderates the overall GI slightly. GL per 350g serving = (65 × 51.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 33.2. This is classified HIGH per standard GL thresholds (GL ≥ 20 = high).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~650mg per plate (~186mg per 100g), primarily from peanut sauce and kecap manis sweet soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: One plate of Ketoprak provides approximately 650mg sodium (~32% of the WHO 2g daily sodium limit); hypertensive patients should request reduced kecap manis and peanut sauce, or limit to a half portion, to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~33.2 per plate) driven by lontong rice cake (GI ~82) and rice vermicelli starchy base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ketoprak is unsuitable for T2DM patients as a regular meal choice due to its very high GL (~33.2 per plate, equivalent to approximately two bowls of steamed rice); patients should prefer Gado Gado (GL ~14) when available, or if only Ketoprak is available, request lontong on the side and consume only half the rice cake portion (~40g) to approximately halve the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (5.1g fat per 100g, ~18g per plate) from peanut sauce, with low saturated fat (1.0g per 100g); sodium load is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ketoprak's peanut sauce provides predominantly unsaturated fats (MUFA/PUFA) which are not inherently harmful for cardiovascular health, but the ~650mg sodium per plate and high glycemic load contribute to cardiovascular risk; CVD patients should limit portion size and request reduced kecap manis to lower sodium exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ketoprak is a traditional Betawi street food originating from Jakarta, Indonesia. It is a rice cake salad composed of lontong (compressed boiled rice cake), rice vermicelli, fried tofu, fresh bean sprouts, and sliced cucumber, all dressed with a rich peanut sauce (bumbu kacang) and kecap manis (Indonesian sweet soy sauce). The dish is served at room temperature and is commonly found at street stalls and Indonesian restaurants across Singapore and Jakarta. The texture is a combination of soft and chewy rice cake, silky noodles, crisp bean sprouts, and creamy peanut sauce. Despite its 'salad' framing, Ketoprak is nutritionally more similar to a noodle dish than a vegetable salad due to its dominant starchy base. Per plate (~350g): approximately 430 kcal, 10g protein, 18g fat, 55g carbohydrate, 4g dietary fibre, and ~650mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium) and the glycemic load per plate is ~33.2 (HIGH) — significantly higher than Gado Gado (GL ~14), which uses a vegetable base rather than rice cake and noodles. CLINICAL DISTINCTION: T2DM patients should prefer Gado Gado over Ketoprak. If only Ketoprak is available, requesting lontong on the side and consuming only half the rice cake portion (~40g) approximately halves the glycemic load. The peanut sauce provides MUFA and PUFA but also contributes to caloric density. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020, an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for lontong rice cake and rice vermicelli with documented estimation basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI values are complete and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Ketoprak as a composite dish is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 for this dish. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 — no hydrogenated fats in traditional preparation). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — dish is plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — dish contains no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — no significant vitamin D sources in this plant-based dish). GI ~65 is an analogous estimate based on weighted starchy component contributions (lontong GI ~82, rice vermicelli GI ~58) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite GI measurement for Ketoprak exists in the published literature as of 2024. GL per serving = (65 × 51.0g available carbohydrate per 350g plate) / 100 = 33.2. The 'salad' descriptor in the dish name is nutritionally misleading — Ketoprak is glycemically equivalent to a noodle dish. FoodEx2 code A0GCG used as closest parent match; no exact Ketoprak entry in FoodEx2.
